summary:
Pegloticase is FDA-approved exclusively for treating chronic gout that is refractory to standard therapies, requiring documented flare history and presence of tophi. It has no role in psoriatic arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, or paralytic ileus due to lack of mechanistic relevance and absent clinical trial support. 

Note: This summary focuses only on clinically significant facts related to the drug’s actual indications per available medical guidelines.

The provided information supports that pegloticase is specifically indicated for chronic gout refractory to conventional treatments, including those with recurrent flares and visible tophi. It does not treat psoriatic arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, or paralytic ileus because these conditions do not align with its mechanism of action or proven efficacy profile.  

(Note: Only two sentence summaries required.)

Pegloticase is indicated solely for chronic gout refractory to standard therapy, particularly when accompanied by frequent flares and subcutaneous tophi. It lacks therapeutic benefit or approval for psoriatic arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, or paralytic ileus due to non-overlapping mechanisms and insufficient clinical evidence.  
Only one response should appear at end; ensure it matches exactly what was requested—two concise sentences focusing strictly on core clinical points regarding the medication's uses.
